name: Inner-City Muslim Action Network
mission: IMAN incorporated as a nonprofit in 1997 through the drive of people directly affected by and deeply invested in social issues affecting communities of color living on Chicago’s South Side. Since that time, IMAN has steadily grown and, in 2016, opened a second office in Atlanta to continue mobilizing a cross-section of people committed to this mission.
theoryOfChange: IMAN uses a holistic, integrative approach combining direct services (health, housing) with systemic change (organizing, advocacy) and cultural work (arts) to address structural injustices.
scoreSummary: INNER-CITY MUSLIM ACTION NETWORK shows good impact and good alignment, with zakat compliance (minor risk deductions applied).

evaluationSources: MacArthur Foundation Grant (to Executive Director Rami Nashashibi) (John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation)

zakatClaimEvidence: Dedicated zakat page found at https://imancentral.org/donate (Source: https://imancentral.org/donate)

all_citations.claim: IMAN provides health services and reentry programs for the community.
IMAN is Zakat-eligible and accepts donations for specific asnaf categories.
The organization offers multiple giving methods and donation options for supporters.
IMAN reported total revenue of $18,475,567, a program expense ratio of 65.9%, 9.9 months of working capital, and a fundraising efficiency of $0.02 per $1 raised.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: IMAN earns a 64/100 score due to its direct impact on domestic poverty and strong financial efficiency, though its program expense ratio suggests room for operational optimization.

dimension_explanations.alignment: The organization shows moderate alignment with a 32/50 score, reflecting its focus on urgent domestic poverty issues and its status as a Zakat-eligible institution [2]. Donors can contribute through various methods, including Zakat-specific funds that support eligible asnaf categories within urban communities [2][3].
dimension_explanations.impact: IMAN demonstrates a direct impact on domestic poverty through its Green ReEntry and health programs, achieving a 34/50 impact score [1][5]. While the organization maintains a healthy 9.9 months of working capital, its program expense ratio is 65.9%, indicating that a significant portion of its $18,475,567 total revenue is directed toward administrative or supporting costs [5].

baseline_narrative.headline: The Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) fosters health, wellness, and healing through holistic community organizing and direct services for returning citizens in Chicago and Atlanta.
strengths: High fundraising efficiency with a cost of only $0.02 per $1 raised
Strong liquidity with 9.9 months of working capital available
Direct service model providing tangible housing and health outcomes
baseline_narrative.summary: IMAN provides critical transitional housing, life skills education, and sustainable construction training for formerly incarcerated individuals [5]. The organization operates a community health center and the Green ReEntry program to address systemic poverty and recidivism [1][5].

confidence.notes: Strong financial data but transparency gaps exist in third-party reviews.

alignment.explanation: IMAN offers a <cite id="7">verified zakat program</cite> and serves highly underserved populations in the US, aligning well with donors focused on domestic poverty and social justice.
alignment.improvement: Alignment could be improved by <cite id="20">clarifying the specific zakat allocation policy</cite> and eligible use categories for donors.

credibility.explanation: IMAN is a well-established institution with <cite id="1">nearly 30 years of history</cite> and a <cite id="12">fully independent board</cite>. However, credibility is tempered by the fact that it <cite id="16">does not currently meet BBB accountability standards</cite>.
credibility.improvement: To strengthen credibility, the organization should <cite id="18">expand its board size</cite> beyond the current 4 members and complete a full BBB Wise Giving Alliance review.

impact.explanation: The organization provides <cite id="2">direct services</cite> including housing and health, which are high-impact interventions. It maintains a <cite id="15">65.9% program ratio</cite> and has <cite id="6">strong liquidity</cite>.
impact.improvement: Impact reporting would benefit from <cite id="19">publishing consistent beneficiary counts</cite> to allow for cost-per-beneficiary analysis.

donor_fit_matrix.evidence_rigor: Grade B - Verified outcomes with documented theory of change

grantmaking_profile.grant_strategy: Direct service provider rather than a grantmaker

rich_narrative.headline: The Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) fosters health, wellness, and healing through holistic community organizing and direct services for returning citizens in Chicago and Atlanta.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who prioritize domestic social justice and want to support holistic, faith-inspired reentry services for formerly incarcerated individuals in major US cities.
donor_motivations: Want to address recidivism and urban poverty in the US
Value the intersection of environmental sustainability and social work
Seek to fulfill zakat through domestic community development
giving_considerations: The organization is currently navigating a period of revenue contraction
Program expense ratios are lower than some international relief peers
Focus is localized to specific urban centers (Chicago and Atlanta)
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking international humanitarian relief or those who require a large, multi-national board of directors for governance assurance.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: The organization has a documented theory of change and verified outcomes, though it lacks independent third-party cost-effectiveness evaluations.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: IMAN creates impact by providing a 'Green ReEntry' pathway that combines housing, vocational training, and health services to break the cycle of recidivism.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that the complex needs of returning citizens are being met effectively through integrated services.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: Recent revenue declines suggest a significant need for new donor support to maintain current service levels.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Unique focus on <cite id="11">green construction training</cite> for formerly incarcerated individuals within a Muslim-led framework.

strengths.detail: The organization demonstrates remarkable efficiency in its development efforts, spending only <cite id="8">$0.03 to raise every $1</cite> in contribution revenue.
IMAN maintains <cite id="9">9.9 months of working capital</cite> in working capital, providing a significant buffer against funding fluctuations.
Having served the community <cite id="1">since 1997</cite>, IMAN has established a deep track record in <cite id="10">Chicago and Atlanta</cite>.

strengths_deep_dive: IMAN's strength lies in its multi-faceted approach to urban revitalization. Its 'Green ReEntry' program provides <cite id="11">sustainable construction training</cite> which simultaneously addresses housing needs and vocational gaps for returning citizens. This is supported by a <cite id="12">100% independent board of directors</cite>, ensuring high levels of internal governance and oversight. Financially, the organization is anchored by <cite id="13">$12,509,163 in public contributions</cite>, supplemented by <cite id="14">$2,979,023 in program service revenue</cite>, indicating a diversified funding base that includes earned income from its health and housing services.
rich_narrative.summary: The Inner-City Muslim Action Network (IMAN) is a community-based organization that has operated since <cite id="1">its founding in 1997</cite> to address systemic poverty and recidivism in Chicago and Atlanta. The organization utilizes a holistic model that integrates <cite id="2">transitional housing, life skills education, and sustainable construction training</cite> for formerly incarcerated individuals. IMAN's operations are substantial, with <cite id="3">total revenue reaching $15,923,414</cite> in the 2023 tax year. Beyond reentry services, the organization maintains a <cite id="4">community health center and a food ecosystem</cite> designed to provide wellness and healing in underserved urban areas. While the organization has faced financial volatility, evidenced by a <cite id="5">3-year revenue CAGR of -21.1%</cite>, it maintains a resilient balance sheet with <cite id="6">9.9 months of working capital</cite> to sustain its direct service delivery. IMAN is <cite id="7">verified as zakat-eligible</cite>, serving specific categories such as the poor (fuqara) and needy (masakin) through its social service programs.
